macro_segments.title: The Case of Unknown Firstborn Twins: Mishnah Introduction and Basic Scenarios

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: Wife bears two male sons without prior births—obligation to give five sela'im to Kohen
One son dies within thirty days: father exempt from Pidyon HaBen
Father dies, children remain: obligation transfers to sons; each must redeem unless they can shift burden
Mishnah dispute: Rebbi Meir on payment before vs. after estate division; Rebbi Yehuda on property lien
Male and female twins: no obligation because firstborn might be female

macro_segments.title: Gemara: When Did the Father Become Obligated? The Timing Problem and Property Lien Doctrine

micro_segments.title: Gemara question: If father died after day 30, when did obligation arise? Lien should persist after division
Rava's objection: liens are not independent rights; must go through obligated person's personal obligation
Proposed reading via two Yosef ben Shimons case: creditor may collect from shared property when individual is unknown
Illustration: loan sale to Charlie and personal waiver (mo'chel)—lien cannot exist independent of debt

macro_segments.title: Resolving the Mishnah: Father Dies After Day 30; Small Estate Creates Divided Ownership Problem

micro_segments.title: Rava's resolution: father must have died after day 30, creating lien on estate; question becomes effect of division
If estate is large, Kohen collects entire five sela'im via lien; case concerns small estate worth only five sela'im
Rav Assi principle: brothers post-division are half-heirs, half-purchasers of each other's shares
Debt written in Torah vs. written document: no automatic lien unless document; oral debt has no lien on property
Rav Pappa: verbal loan collected from heirs (continuity) but not from buyers; no document = no lien on purchasers

macro_segments.title: The Debate: Can Kohen Collect Half or Must Have Full Five Sela'im?

micro_segments.title: Rebbi Meir: five as indivisible unit—cannot collect if only half available after division
Rebbi Yehuda: can collect even half; property is obligated even after division
Baraita: Rebbi Yehuda requires full twenty zuz (five sela'im) across both brothers to trigger obligation
Gemara resolution: both agree full five required; debate is whether division allows collection of full amount
Rebbi Yehuda allows full collection by rejecting assumptions: either Torah-debt creates lien, or post-division son is full heir

macro_segments.title: Alternative Sugya Path: Starting from Rebbi Yehuda's Position and Rava's Property Doctrine

micro_segments.title: Some texts discuss same issue starting from Rebbi Yehuda's clause rather than Rebbi Meir's
Two Yosef ben Shimons revisited with purchase element: one brother buys field from other; creditor collects via lien
Rava rejects this approach: cannot go straight to property; obligation must be proven against specific person
Conclusion: ritual pidyon obligation derives from civil loan obligation; without collectible debt, no mitzvah

topical_tags.term: Rebbi Meir vs. Rebbi Yehuda dispute
Lien doctrine: property as guarantor, not independent of person
Two Yosef ben Shimons case (two people with same name)
Rav Assi: half-heir, half-purchaser after division
Debt written in Torah vs. written in document (shtar)
Rav Pappa: verbal loan collected from heirs, not from buyers
Chamesh v'lo chatzi chamesh (five, not half-five)
Gemara's conceptual distinction: ritual obligation hinges on civil liability
Yesh bereira (retroactive definition via division outcome)
